content: Let me read the necessary files to complete this blueprint review task. I'll start by reading the instructions and project context files, then dive into the specific chapters mentioned in the directive.
I'll read the key files in parallel to conduct this blueprint audit efficiently.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
130	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
131	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
132	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
133	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
134	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
135	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
136	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
137	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
138	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
139	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
140	
141	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
142	
143	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
144	
145	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
146	
147	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
148	
149	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
150	
151	## Always read everything
152	
153	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
154	
155	## Unstarted-phase proposals
156	
157	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
158	
159	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
160	
161	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
162	
163	The proposal answers:
164	
165	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
166	
167	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
168	
169	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
170	
171	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
172	
173	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
174	
175	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
176	
177	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
178	
179	```
180	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
181	
182	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
183	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
184	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
185	
186	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
187	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
188	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
189	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
190	...
191	
192	**`\uses` skeleton**:
193	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
194	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
195	
196	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
197	
198	**References for writer**:
199	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
200	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
201	
202	**Subphase choices exposed**:
203	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
204	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
205	```
206	
207	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
208	
209	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
210	
211	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
212	
213	## Directive Format
214	
215	```markdown
216	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
217	
218	## Slug
219	<slug>
220	
221	## Strategy snapshot
222	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
223	
224	## Routes
225	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
226	
227	## References
228	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
229	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
230	
231	## Focus areas (optional)
232	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
233	
234	## Known issues
235	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
236	```
237	
238	## What you do
239	
240	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
241	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
242	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
243	   - Read the entire chapter.
244	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
245	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
246	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
247	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
248	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
249	6. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
250	7. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
251	8. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
252	
253	You may also use:
254	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
255	- `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" .` — quick view of the project's Lean ↔ blueprint dependency map.
256	
257	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
258	
259	## Report format
260	
261	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
262	
263	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
264	
265	Concretely:
266	
267	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all five are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
268	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
269	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
270	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
271	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
272	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
273	
274	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
275	
276	```markdown
277	# Blueprint Review Report
278	
279	## Slug
280	<slug>
281	
282	## Iteration
283	<NNN>
284	
285	## Top-level summaries
286	
287	### Incomplete parts
288	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
289	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
290	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
291	
292	### Proofs lacking detail
293	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
294	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
295	
296	### Lean difficulty quality
297	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
298	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
299	
300	### Multi-route coverage

366	## Severity summary
367	
368	Apply these rules verbatim:
369	
370	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
371	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.

388	The severity classification matters because the plan agent's gate uses it directly: any **must-fix-this-iter** finding tied to a chapter prevents the corresponding prover from running this iter. Unstarted-phase proposals do not block existing provers — they trigger blueprint-writer dispatch for future prover work. Do not under-classify them as "informational"; the project pays in deferred parallelism every iter a phase sits without a blueprint.

400	## Reminders
401	
402	- **Read every chapter, no scope shortcuts.**
403	- **Cross-reference every strategy phase against existing chapters.** An unstarted phase is not a clean bill of health — it is an opportunity to write the blueprint now and enable parallelism.
404	- **Proposals are actionable, not informational.** Each proposal block must be concrete enough to serve as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Vague "chapter needed for X" notes are not proposals.

406	- **You audit against the directive's context**, not your own ideas of what the project should look like — but you ARE critical of weak prose and under-specified Lean hints.
407	- **You flag, you don't fix.** Even when the fix is obvious, the plan agent decides what changes next iter.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

2236	  The proof runs in three movements: the d.1-bridge turns membership in \(J.W\) into a
2237	  stalkwise statement, the second-factor naturality of the d.2 isomorphism transports
2238	  the whiskered stalk into a tensor of the stalk maps, and a flatness-free

2241	  \emph{(a) The d.1-bridge.} Specialise to the topological site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
2242	  where stalks exist. By the hypothesis \(g \in J.W\) and the d.1-bridge
2243	  \cref{lem:W_implies_stalkwise_iso}, the \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map \(g_x : M_x \to N_x\)
2244	  is an isomorphism at every point \(x \in X\). By \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map} this
2245	  stalk map carries an \(R_x\)-linear structure, and the bijectivity just obtained
2246	  upgrades it to an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2247	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso} : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\).
2248	
2249	  \emph{(b) Identifying the whiskered stalk.} By the second-factor naturality of the
2250	  stalk--tensor comparison \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation_naturality_right}, the
2251	  isomorphism \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\) of \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
2252	  identifies the stalk \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) of the whiskered morphism with the
2253	  map
2254	  \[
2255	    \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x \;=\; \mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x
2256	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \longrightarrow F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .

2260	  \(g_x : M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) of (a) on the left by the identity of \(F_x\)
2261	  yields an \(R_x\)-linear equivalence
2262	  \(\mathtt{LinearMap.lTensor}\,F_x\,g_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\)

2286	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
2287	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
2288	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
2289	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
2290	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
2291	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
2292	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
2293	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
2294	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
2295	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
2296	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}\leanok
111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  \leanok
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	\leanok
191	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
192	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
193	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
194	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
195	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
196	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
197	\end{proof}
198	
199	\begin{lemma}
200	
201	\leanok
202	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
203	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
204	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
206	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
207	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
208	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
209	  \[
210	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
211	    \;\cong\;
212	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
213	  \]
214	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
215	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
216	  of \(N\).
217	\end{lemma}
218	
219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
221	  \leanok
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  \leanok
253	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
254	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
255	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
256	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
257	\end{proof}
258	
259	\begin{lemma}
260	% NOTE: iter-237 — only the CONDITIONAL form was formalized this iter:
261	% `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule` (axiom-clean), which takes the
262	% per-basic-open localization fact `hloc` as an explicit hypothesis. The unconditional decl named in
263	% the `\lean{}` pin below does NOT yet exist (pin is currently dangling). The sole remaining obligation
264	% is the carrier-wall `hloc` (structure-sheaf smul identity on the pushforward sections over D(a)).
265	% Two route-iii helpers built this iter also lack pins: `fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule`
266	% (section-level engine) and `IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars` (ring-change core).
267	% Plan agent: add `\lean{}` blocks for those two helpers + the conditional decl, and decide whether to
268	% repoint this pin. See task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-fbc.md.
269	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
270	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
271	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
272	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
273	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
274	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
275	  \[
276	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
277	    \;\cong\;
278	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
279	  \]
280	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
281	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
282	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
283	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
284	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
285	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
286	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
287	
288	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
289	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
290	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
291	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
292	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
293	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
294	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
295	\end{lemma}
296	
297	\begin{proof}
298	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
299	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
300	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
301	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
302	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
303	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
304	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
305	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
306	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
307	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
308	
309	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
310	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
311	  \[
312	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
313	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
314	  \]
315	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
316	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
317	
318	  \medskip
319	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
320	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
321	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
322	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
323	
324	  \medskip
325	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
326	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
327	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
328	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
329	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
330	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
331	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
332	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
333	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
334	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
335	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
336	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
337	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
338	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
339	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
340	  an isomorphism.
341	
342	  \medskip
343	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
344	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
345	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
346	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
347	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
348	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
349	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a
350	  consequence — no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' input is
351	  needed for the affine lane.
352	\end{proof}
353	
354	\begin{remark}
355	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
356	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
357	  \begin{enumerate}
358	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
359	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
360	      \(R\)-module of sections of
361	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
362	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
363	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
364	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
365	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
366	      closed under isomorphism; hence
367	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
368	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
369	      needed for the affine lane.
370	  \end{enumerate}
371	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
372	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
373	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
374	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
375	\end{remark}
376	
377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
378	[Affine base change]
379	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
381	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
382	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
383	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
384	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
385	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
386	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
387	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
388	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
389	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
390	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
391	  % we have
392	  % $$
393	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
394	  % $$"
395	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
396	  change''.}
397	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
398	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
399	  above the canonical map of
400	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
401	  \[
402	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
403	  \]
404	  The quasi-coherence hypothesis on \(\mathcal{F}\) is essential and is not a mere
405	  convenience: the proof works over an affine open by writing
406	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for a module \(M\), and a general
407	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form
408	  \(\widetilde{M}\); without it neither the affine description of the pushforward
409	  nor the tensor-associativity computation below is available, and the conclusion
410	  fails.
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
414	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
415	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
416	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
417	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
418	% for some $A$-module $M$.
419	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
420	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
421	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
422	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
423	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
424	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
425	% equality
426	% $$
427	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
428	% $$
429	% as $R'$-modules."
430	% NOTE: Lean proof in progress. The locality reduction
431	% (lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens) lands; the affine computation is reframed
432	% around full-faithfulness of the tilde functor (essential image = the
433	% quasi-coherent modules), which collapses "iso of Spec-modules" to "iso of a
434	% concrete ModuleCat R' map" and avoids all section-level SMul/Module-instance
435	% plumbing. The single Mathlib-absent ingredient is
436	% lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (next FlatBaseChange prover target); the closing
437	% algebra is TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange, present in
438	% Mathlib with no flatness. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
439	\begin{proof}
440	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
441	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
442	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
443	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
444	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). Fix one such affine
445	  open. The assertion is then local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all
446	  schemes in sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
447	  \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\)
448	  corresponding to a ring map \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the
449	  quasi-coherent sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that
450	  quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) is used: it is what licenses writing
451	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) over the affine open.) Computing the fibre product
452	  gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
453	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
454	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). In this affine
455	  situation the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
456	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
457	
458	  \medskip
459	  \emph{Reduction to a concrete module map via full-faithfulness of tilde.} The
460	  functor \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
461	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful, with essential
462	  image precisely the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently, the counit comparison
463	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
464	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
465	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms; by naturality of the counit, a
466	  square relates \(\alpha\) to \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\alpha)}\), and since the two
467	  vertical counit maps are isomorphisms,
468	  \[
469	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
470	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
471	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
472	  \]
473	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map of
474	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). This reframing keeps the whole computation at
475	  the level of modules and morphisms of affine schemes; in particular it requires
476	  no manipulation of section-level scalar-multiplication or module instances. The
477	  ring map underlying the pushforward at the top open is the global-sections map
478	  \(f_{\top}\) of the morphism (which for \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
479	  conjugate to \(\varphi\) via the naturality of the \(\Gamma\)--\(\operatorname{Spec}\)
480	  unit), and the preimage of the top open under \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is
481	  again the top open, so the identification of sections needs no transport. Any
482	  residual scalar identity in \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) is settled by the
483	  scalar-tower compatibility of restriction of scalars.
484	
485	  \medskip
486	  \emph{Identification of the concrete map.} Under
487	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, pushforward along the affine morphism
488	  is restriction of scalars on modules and pullback along \(g\) is
489	  \(- \otimes_R R'\); accordingly \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is identified with the
490	  canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison map between the two ways of forming the
491	  iterated tensor product. The claim therefore reduces to the associativity /
492	  cancellation isomorphism
493	  \[
494	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\cong\; R' \otimes_R M
495	  \]
496	  of \(R'\)-modules, which is an isomorphism (no flatness is needed in the affine
497	  case).
498	\end{proof}

502	\begin{theorem}\leanok
503	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
504	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
505	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
506	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
507	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
508	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
509	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
510	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
511	  % $$
512	  % \xymatrix{
513	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
514	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
515	  % }
516	  % $$
517	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
518	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
519	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
520	  % For any $i \geq 0$
521	  % \begin{enumerate}
522	  % \item the base change map of
523	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
524	  % is an isomorphism
525	  % $$
526	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
527	  % $$
528	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
529	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
530	  % \end{enumerate}"
531	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
532	  \(i = 0\) case.}
533	  Consider the cartesian square
534	  \[
535	    \begin{array}{ccc}
536	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
537	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
538	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
539	    \end{array}
540	  \]
541	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
542	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
543	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
544	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
545	  \[
546	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
547	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
548	  \]
549	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
550	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
551	  \[
552	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
553	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
554	  \]
555	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
556	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
557	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
558	
559	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
560	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
561	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
562	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
563	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
564	  an isomorphism.
565	\end{theorem}

617	% NOTE: Lean proof is a documented sorry (deferred). Its affine input is the
618	% affine lemma above, which in turn reduces to lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (the
619	% affine-pushforward-of-tilde brick) plus the tilde full-faithfulness reframe. On
620	% top of that it needs Čech-cohomology / affine-cover infrastructure for
621	% SheafOfModules, which Mathlib does not provide. This remains a multi-lane engine
622	% build, not a single-iter target. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
623	\begin{proof}
624	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
625	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
626	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
627	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
628	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
629	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
630	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
631	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
632	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
633	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
634	  \(B\)-modules
635	  \[
636	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
637	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
638	  \]
639	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
640	
641	  \medskip
642	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
643	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
644	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
645	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
646	  computes sheaf cohomology:
647	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
648	  the cover yields an affine open cover
649	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
650	  \(X_B\), with the same property
651	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
652	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
653	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
654	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
655	  \[
656	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
657	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
658	  \]
659	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
660	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
661	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
662	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
663	
664	  \medskip
665	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
666	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
667	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
668	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
669	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
670	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
671	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
672	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
673	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
674	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
675	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
676	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
677	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
678	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
679	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
680	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
681	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
682	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
683	\end{proof}
